title: Benchmarking Foundation Models with Retrieval-Augmented Generation in Olympic-Level Physics Problem Solving
problem_statement: The capacity of retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) with foundation models for expert-level reasoning, such as solving Olympiad-level physics problems, remains largely unexplored. It proposes a training-free method that leverages retrieval-augmented generation and LLM code generation to query graph databases. This approach aims to handle diverse graph reasoning tasks efficiently and robustly.

title: Beamforming-LLM: What, Where and When Did I Miss?
problem_statement: Users in multi-speaker environments often miss parts of conversations and need a way to semantically recall what they missed. The core technical problem is to capture, process, and retrieve missed conversational segments from directional audio streams using natural language queries. This requires integrating spatial audio capture, retrieval-augmented generation, and summarization to provide context-aware auditory memory.

title: From Rows to Reasoning: A Retrieval-Augmented Multimodal Framework for Spreadsheet Understanding
problem_statement: Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle to reason over large-scale enterprise spreadsheets containing thousands of numeric rows, multiple linked sheets, and embedded visual content such as charts and receipts. Prior approaches rely on single-sheet compression or full-context encoding, which limits scalability and fails to reflect real user interaction with complex, multimodal workbooks.

explicit_limitations.name: Scalability of prior methods
explicit_limitations.description: Prior state-of-the-art spreadsheet reasoning approaches rely on single-sheet compression or full-context encoding, which limits scalability for large workbooks.
evidence_phrases: Prior state-of-the-art spreadsheet reasoning approaches typically rely on single-sheet compression or full-context encoding, which limits scalability

evidence_refs.quote: Prior state-of-the-art spreadsheet reasoning approaches typically rely on single-sheet compression or full-context encoding, which limits scalability

core_ideas.name: FRTR-Bench benchmark
core_ideas.mechanism: Introduces a large-scale benchmark for multimodal spreadsheet reasoning, comprising 30 enterprise-grade Excel workbooks spanning nearly four million cells and more than 50 embedded images.
evidence_phrases: We introduce FRTR-Bench, the first large-scale benchmark for multimodal spreadsheet reasoning, comprising 30 enterprise-grade Excel workbooks spanning nearly four million cells and more than 50 embedded images.

evidence_refs.quote: We introduce FRTR-Bench, the first large-scale benchmark for multimodal spreadsheet reasoning, comprising 30 enterprise-grade Excel workbooks spanning nearly four million cells and more than 50 embedded images.

core_ideas.name: FRTR framework
core_ideas.mechanism: Decomposes Excel workbooks into granular row, column, and block embeddings, employs hybrid lexical-dense retrieval with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF), and integrates multimodal embeddings to reason over both numerical and visual information.
evidence_phrases: We present From Rows to Reasoning (FRTR), an advanced, multimodal retrieval-augmented generation framework that decomposes Excel workbooks into granular row, column, and block embeddings, employs hybrid lexical-dense retrieval with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF), and integrates multimodal embeddings to reason over both numerical and visual information.

evidence_refs.quote: We present From Rows to Reasoning (FRTR), an advanced, multimodal retrieval-augmented generation framework that decomposes Excel workbooks into granular row, column, and block embeddings, employs hybrid lexical-dense retrieval with Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF), and integrates multimodal embeddings to reason over both numerical and visual information.

experiment_signals.setting: Evaluation on FRTR-Bench and SpreadsheetLLM benchmark using six LLMs
experiment_signals.claim: FRTR achieves 74% answer accuracy on FRTR-Bench with Claude Sonnet 4.5, a substantial improvement over prior state-of-the-art approaches that reached only 24%, and 87% accuracy on SpreadsheetLLM benchmark with GPT-5 while reducing token usage by roughly 50% compared to direct serialization.
evidence_phrases: We tested FRTR on six LLMs, achieving 74% answer accuracy on FRTR-Bench with Claude Sonnet 4.5, a substantial improvement over prior state-of-the-art approaches that reached only 24%. On the SpreadsheetLLM benchmark, FRTR achieved 87% accuracy with GPT-5 while reducing token usage by roughly 50% compared to direct serialization methods.

evidence_refs.quote: We tested FRTR on six LLMs, achieving 74% answer accuracy on FRTR-Bench with Claude Sonnet 4.5, a substantial improvement over prior state-of-the-art approaches that reached only 24%. On the SpreadsheetLLM benchmark, FRTR achieved 87% accuracy with GPT-5 while reducing token usage by roughly 50% compared to direct serialization methods.

title: RAGdb: A Zero-Dependency, Embeddable Architecture for Multimodal Retrieval-Augmented Generation on the Edge
problem_statement: The prevailing RAG architecture has evolved into a complex, distributed stack requiring cloud-hosted vector databases, heavy deep learning frameworks, and high-latency embedding inference servers. This creates a significant barrier to entry for edge computing, air-gapped environments, and privacy-constrained applications where data sovereignty is paramount.

title: Exploration of Augmentation Strategies in Multi-modal Retrieval-Augmented Generation for the Biomedical Domain: A Case Study Evaluating Question Answering in Glycobiology
problem_statement: The paper addresses the trade-off in multi-modal retrieval-augmented generation (MM-RAG) for biomedical question answering between converting figures/tables into text versus using OCR-free visual retrieval that returns page images.
